orig_title: Introducción · Filosofía Estratégica
title: Introduction · Strategic Philosophy
body:
Brawl Stars is, on the surface, a fast-paced action shooter with simple mechanics. However, beneath that surface lies a game of deep strategic layers: every match is a real-time optimization problem where the map, game modes, your team composition, and positioning decisions continuously interact.

This guide is not a tier list or a Brawler ranking. Its goal is to give you the thinking framework with which you can analyze any in-game situation on your own: understanding why certain Brawlers dominate certain maps, why a team composition works or fails, and how you should behave when you're on a random, uncoordinated team.

Who is this guide aimed at?
→ Players in Gold–Diamond rank who want to improve their understanding of the game.
→ Players who feel like they're 'doing things right' but still lose matches they don't understand.
→ Competitive players who want to build their own analytical framework.
→ Anyone curious about the theory behind multiplayer game design.

The Three Pillars of Strategic Thinking in Brawl Stars

🗺️ THE MAP Defines which Brawlers are useful and which positions hold value. The map is the board; ignoring it means playing blind. 🎯 THE OBJECTIVE Every game mode has a unique win condition. Kills, gems, balls, or zones: confusing the objective is the number one cause of defeats. 👥 YOUR ROLE ON THE TEAM Your Brawler doesn't act alone. Understanding what your team lacks and how you complement it is the most valuable strategic skill.

This guide is organized into four sections: Fundamentals (what roles, maps, and modes are), Team Theory (how to build and read compositions), Practical Application (in-match decisions), and Meta & Adaptation (how the game evolves). Each section can be read independently, but the greatest benefit is gained by following the proposed order.

Sections will be added to this document progressively.

orig_title: ¿Qué es un Rol?
title: What is a Role?
body:
A role is the function that a Brawler performs optimally given its attack mechanics, range, mobility, special abilities, and base stats. The same Brawler can fulfill different roles depending on the map and game mode, but will always have a primary role that defines its tactical identity.

Key concept: Tactical identity
The tactical identity of a Brawler is the answer to the question 'what problem does this Brawler solve on a team?' If you cannot answer it concisely, you are not reading the Brawler correctly. Examples: Mortis 'eliminates supports in advanced positions'; Poco 'keeps allies alive in prolonged combat'; Piper 'punishes enemies who expose themselves in open spaces'.

orig_title: Las Variables que Definen un Mapa
title: The Variables That Define a Map
body:
Every Brawl Stars map can be analyzed through five independent variables. The combination of these variables determines which roles are viable, which are optimal, and which are directly ruled out.

# Variable What it measures Impact on roles
1 Cover density Quantity and distribution of walls, bushes, and obstacles per unit area. High density
→ Throwers and Assassins gain value. Open spaces
→ Snipers and long-range DPS dominate.
2 Lane length Average distance between combat zones. Wide maps vs. narrow maps. Long, narrow lanes favor Snipers. Short, wide lanes favor Tanks and area DPS.
3 Center control Degree to which the central point of the map is contested and strategically relevant. Contested center
→ Controllers and Supports gain value. Irrelevant center
→ gameplay becomes more individual.
4 Bush distribution Position, size, and connectivity of tall grass areas. Bushes in lanes
→ Assassins and Tanks can ambush. Bushes on flanks
→ mobility and flank pressure.
5 Map symmetry Degree to which both sides of the map are identical or equivalent. High symmetry
→ mirror compositions. Asymmetry
→ one team may have a structural positional advantage.

orig_title: La Win Condition: el concepto mas importante del apartado
title: The Win Condition: the most important concept of the section
body:
The win condition of a game mode is the minimum and sufficient action that guarantees victory. It is not the objective the game shows on screen, but the chain of decisions that leads to that objective consistently.

Example: Gem Grab
On-screen objective: Collect 10 gems and hold the counter for 15 seconds. Real win condition: Control the center of the map long enough to accumulate gems, and the moment you have 10 or more, fall back to a defensive position and protect the carrier. The difference: Many teams keep attacking when they already have 10 gems, risking losing them all. The real win condition requires switching from aggressive mode to defensive mode at a precise moment.

orig_title: El concepto de Cobertura de Roles
title: The concept of Role Coverage
body:
A team covers a role when at least one of its three Brawlers can fulfill that function as a primary or secondary role. Coverage does not require the role to be perfect: it requires that the team has no critical functional gap that the opponent can exploit.

CRITICAL functional gap TOLERABLE functional gap
No one to sustain damage in Hot Zone: the team cannot stay in the zone under pressure. No one to heal in Gem Grab with an active counter: the gem carrier dies as soon as they are focused. No short-range DPS on a closed Heist map: the team cannot clear defenders. No Assassin in Gem Grab: the team loses flanking mobility but can compensate with zone pressure. No Thrower in Bounty: the team loses damage over cover but can compensate with Snipers in open space. No Support in Brawl Ball: tolerable if the team has enough burst to close goals quickly.

orig_title: Que define el arquetipo de una composicion
title: What defines the archetype of a composition
body:
Each composition has a tactical identity that determines how it seeks to win the match. That identity is defined by three axes:

Axis Extreme A Extreme B
1 Game pace Aggressive — the team seeks to eliminate opponents to create a numerical advantage that allows them to execute the objective. Passive — the team holds out, denies space, and waits for the opponent to make mistakes in order to capitalize.
2 Resource focus Objective — all resources (damage, positioning, Supers) are directed toward the mode's objective. Kills — resources are directed toward eliminating opponents, expecting that deaths will create openings toward the objective.
3 Combat range Short — the team seeks to close distances and fight within the opponent's cover. Long — the team maintains distance and punishes opponents who expose themselves.

orig_title: Los cuatro tipos de sinergia
title: The four types of synergy
body:
Every synergy between Brawlers can be classified into one of these four types. Identifying the type lets you know under what conditions it activates and when it stops working.

Type 1 — Enable Synergy One Brawler creates the necessary condition for another to execute their highest-value action. Without the first, the second cannot act effectively. With the first, the second has a guaranteed chance of success. Examples: Sandy puts multiple enemies to sleep — Leon moves in and eliminates the high-value target. Jessie places Scrappy in the center — the turret forces enemies to expose themselves — an ally makes the pick.
Type 2 — Amplification Synergy (Buff) One Brawler directly increases the damage, speed, survivability, or range of another. The second Brawler already works well alone, but with the first they reach a qualitatively superior level of effectiveness. Examples: Max speeds up the entire team — Assassins can move in and out faster. Byron heals and amplifies an ally's damage — any DPS becomes a greater threat.
Type 3 — Mutual Coverage Synergy (Anti-weakness) Two Brawlers that individually have opposite weaknesses protect each other. The first covers the second's weakness and vice versa, creating a pair more robust than either of them alone. Examples: Piper (vulnerable at close range) + Bull (eliminates everything that approaches Piper). Barley (slow and without mobility) + Mortis (intercepts any Assassin that flanks the Thrower).
Type 4 — Super Chain Synergy One Brawler charges another's Super through their attack mechanic or gameplay, creating Super chains that activate rapidly. In modes where Supers are decisive, charging speed is an enormous tactical advantage. Examples: Nita summons Bruce, Bruce receives shots and charges Nita's Super quickly. Jacky draws enemies toward her with her area attack, charging the Super of the allied Tank who needs to hit multiple targets.

orig_title: El proceso de lectura: cinco preguntas en orden
title: The reading process: five questions in order
body:
Reading the role in a random team is not a single question but a chain of five questions answered in order. Each answer conditions the next. The complete process takes less than ten seconds once internalized.

Q Question Why it matters and what it determines
Q1 What is the win condition of this mode? It is the starting point. Everything else —position, resources, role— must be oriented toward executing that win condition. If you forget it, you make decisions that seem locally correct but are incorrect at the match level.
Q2 What map archetype is this and what roles does it favor? Defines the structural context. A closed map demands different behaviors than a lane map. Your role can change completely depending on the map even if your Brawler is the same.
Q3 What roles do my allies cover? Identifies the roles present in the team. Do not analyze whether they are good or bad Brawlers: analyze what function they cover. Look for the gaps: what critical role is missing that you must cover or compensate for.
Q4 What role can my Brawler fulfill in this context? Apply the primary and secondary role of your Brawler to the context of the team and the map. Ask: of what the team is missing, what can I contribute given my specific Brawler? The answer is your role on this team.
Q5 What concrete behavior does that role imply? Translate the role into specific actions: where do I position myself, who do I attack first, when do I use the Super, when do I retreat. Without this step, the analysis is theory without practical application.

orig_title: Zonas de valor: no todo el mapa vale lo mismo
title: Value Zones: not every part of the map is worth the same
body:
In any Brawl Stars map, space is not homogeneous. Some positions have much greater tactical value than others because they offer advantageous lines of fire, access to the objective, or control of the enemy's rotation lanes. These are the universal value zones:

Objective Zone The area around the mode's objective: the gem mine, the goal, the safe, the Hot Zone circle.
Who should control it The Tank or the Brawler with the highest survivability on the team. It is the highest-pressure zone, so it requires the profile with the greatest damage tolerance. In countdown modes (Gem Grab, Hot Zone), the resource carrier must stay OUTSIDE this zone once the countdown is activated.
Dominant Flank The side of the map with the most cover, greater access to bushes, or a more favorable firing angle toward the center. In asymmetric maps, there is always one flank that offers more advantage than the other.
Who should control it The Sniper, long-range DPS, or Thrower who can take advantage of the lateral cover without exposing themselves to direct combat in the center. Controlling the dominant flank allows you to pressure the enemy at the objective without entering it.
Bottleneck The mandatory chokepoint between the two halves of the map: a narrow corridor, a bridge, an opening in a wall. Whoever controls the bottleneck controls when and how the enemy can rotate.
Who should control it The zone Controller or the Thrower. The bottleneck is the position where area damage has the most impact because the enemy cannot avoid it without going around the entire map. A Barley or Emz positioned at the bottleneck can deny access to the objective indefinitely.
Flanking Bushes The tall grass areas located in the side lanes or behind the combat lines. They allow movement without being seen and enable ambushes or covert rotations.
Who should control it Assassins and ambush Tanks. Flanking bushes are the natural starting point for covert approaches. Controlling them with your own presence or with preventive damage (shooting into the bush) denies the enemy one of their most dangerous approach routes.

orig_title: La Super: el recurso mas decisivo
title: The Super: the most decisive resource
body:
The super is the most powerful resource of any Brawler and the one with the greatest individual impact on the outcome of a play. But its power is not in using it: it is in using it at the right moment. A super used one second before or after the optimal moment can go from changing the outcome of the match to generating no value at all.

THE SUPER Slow-charging special ability. It charges by attacking or taking damage depending on the Brawler. Activates the Brawler's most powerful effect.
When to hold it When the opponent can easily dodge it (they are moving, have cover, or their position is not optimal for your super's effect). When there is a more important situation in 3-5 seconds: a counter that is about to activate, an Assassin that is about to engage, an allied counter that is active and needs protection. When you have no allies in position to capitalize on your super's effect (Frank stuns but if nobody shoots the stunned opponent, the effect is wasted). When you have low health and need to escape: saving the super to flee is valid if there is no immediate offensive use. When to use it When the opponent is in a fixed position and cannot dodge: the moment of maximum impact of any super is when the target cannot move. When you have a numerical advantage and the super can turn it into a decisive advantage: 3v2 with an area super to eliminate the second opponent before the team can react. When the gem counter or Hot Zone counter is active and the super can disrupt the opponent's position in the zone. When another ally's super has created the window of opportunity: chaining supers is the most powerful play in the game. Most frequent mistake Using the super reflexively shortly after charging it, without waiting for the optimal moment. The impulse to use the super as soon as it charges is one of the most costly habits in the game. A charged and unused super generates tactical pressure on the opponent: the opponent knows you can use it and that modifies their behavior.

orig_title: Los cuatro ejes del matchup
title: The four axes of the matchup
body:
Every matchup between two Brawlers can be analyzed through four independent axes. The advantage in most of these axes determines the outcome of the 1v1 duel under neutral conditions. The map and mode context can alter the weight of each axis.

Axis 1 — Effective range The Brawler who can deal damage to the opponent without being within the range where the opponent deals damage back has a range advantage. This is the most direct and most common form of advantage in a matchup: if I can hit you without you hitting me, the trade always benefits me. **You win if: **your range exceeds the opponent's AND the map gives you enough line of sight to take advantage of it. **You lose if: **the opponent closes the distance or the map denies your line of sight (closed map against a Sniper).
Axis 2 — Damage per second vs. health If the opponent's DPS exceeds your ability to regenerate or heal health faster than you receive damage, the opponent wins the sustained trade. This axis determines who lasts longer in a prolonged fight at equal positioning. **You win if: **your DPS exceeds the opponent's health within the time it takes to complete the full trade without receiving a full response. **You lose if: **the opponent has more health than you can eliminate before their DPS pushes you out of position.
Axis 3 — Mobility and evasion The Brawler with greater mobility can control the combat distance, closing in when convenient and retreating when the trade is unfavorable. Mobility is especially decisive in matchups where one Brawler needs to get into the other's range to be effective. **You win if: **your mobility allows you to enter combat range faster than the opponent can react, or to get out of range before receiving the full response. **You lose if: **the opponent has enough mobility to readjust the distance every time you try to establish the combat range that suits you.
Axis 4 — Super and Gadget in the matchup The Supers and Gadgets of some Brawlers directly counter the opponent's abilities. A Super that interrupts, establishes invulnerability, or denies movement can change the outcome of a matchup that would otherwise be lost. **You win if: **your Super or Gadget neutralizes the opponent's most valuable resource in the duel (Rosa's Super makes her invulnerable for the time the opponent needs to eliminate her; Belle's Super marks the opponent causing the entire team to deal more damage). **You lose if: **the opponent's Super counters your core mechanic (Crow poisons Rosa during her invulnerable Super, negating her passive healing).

orig_title: Que es realmente el meta
title: What is really the meta
body:
The meta is the intersection of three variables that change independently and together define which Brawlers are optimal at any given moment:

Variable 1 Brawler State The Brawler's base stats, super, gadgets and Star Powers in their current version. Each patch can change these values and completely reposition a Brawler in the meta. Variable 2 Maps in rotation The active maps in each mode at any given moment. A Brawler that was mediocre on open maps can become S-tier if the active rotation has a majority of closed maps. Variable 3 Predominant level of play What most players at your rank know and don't know how to do. A technically superior Brawler with a high learning curve can have a worse winrate at lower ranks than a simpler one.

orig_title: BLOQUE I · FUNDAMENTOS
title: BLOCK I · FUNDAMENTALS

orig_title: Los Roles Primarios
title: The Primary Roles
body:
The following describes the fundamental roles that exist in Brawl Stars. These are not closed categories: many Brawlers have a primary role and a secondary role.

Role Description Examples
🛡️ Tank Absorbs damage and creates space for the team. High health, low or medium mobility. Operates at close range. Bull, Darryl, Jacky, Rosa, Frank
🎯 Sniper Deals massive damage at long range. Vulnerable if enemies close the distance. Needs team cover. Piper, Belle, Brock, Colt, 8-Bit
⚔️ Damage Dealer Sustained DPS or high burst at medium range. Versatile, they form the core of most compositions. Shelly, El Primo, Bibi, Colette, Ash
🗡️ Assassin High mobility and explosive damage at close range. Seeks to eliminate isolated targets or enemy supports. Mortis, Leon, Crow, Fang, Edgar
💚 Support Heals, protects, or empowers allies. Essential in zone control modes and extended objective modes. Poco, Byron, Max, Sandy, Janet
💣 Thrower Launches arcing projectiles that ignore walls. Controls zones without a direct line of sight. Highly map-dependent. Dynamike, Barley, Sprout, Tick, Grom
🎮 Controller Controls the map through damage areas, slowdowns, or denial zones. Does not seek kills, but rather positioning. Emz, Lou, Nita, Griff, Eve
🔫 Hybrid Combines characteristics of two roles depending on the context. Harder to read but very versatile. Buzz, Chester, Sam, Kit, Melodie

orig_title: Arquetipos de Mapa
title: Map Archetypes
body:
Although every map in Brawl Stars is unique, they can all be classified into four fundamental archetypes. Knowing the archetype of a map before you start gives you 80% of the tactical information you need to make the right pick.

ARCHETYPE 1 — OPEN MAP (Firing Range)

**Characteristics: **Little to no cover, full visibility from end to end, no bushes covering flanks. Combat almost always occurs with direct line of sight.

✅ Favored roles ❌ Penalized roles
Snipers — maximum range with no obstacles Long-range DPS — sustained damage without risk Controllers with wide projectile areas Throwers — without walls there is no arc angle Assassins — without cover they cannot close in Tanks — exposed before reaching range

**Examples: **Dry Season, Snake Prairie, Double Swoosh (Bounty and Brawl Ball in open versions).

ARCHETYPE 2 — CLOSED MAP (Maze)

**Characteristics: **High density of walls and obstacles, multiple approach angles, short and fragmented lines of sight. Combat occurs at close range in the majority of engagements.

✅ Favored roles ❌ Penalized roles
Throwers — walls are their main tool Assassins — can close in without being seen Tanks — reduced range places enemies within their reach Snipers — without line of sight their damage is irrelevant Long-range DPS — blocked by obstacles Large-area Controllers — insufficient space

**Examples: **Cavern Churn, Skull Creek, Minecart Madness (Heist and Gem Grab maps with high density).

ARCHETYPE 3 — LANE MAP (Corridor)

**Characteristics: **The map is divided into two or three defined horizontal lanes with lateral cover but a passable center. Combat is organized along pressure lines with occasional rotations between lanes.

✅ Favored roles ❌ Penalized roles
Mid-range DPS — constant lane pressure Supports — heal allies in lane without overexposing Throwers — flank from lateral cover Assassins — lanes make covert access difficult Low-mobility Tanks — too slow to rotate

**Examples: **Hard Rock Mine, Acute Angle, Canal Grande (Gem Grab and Hot Zone with defined lanes).

ARCHETYPE 4 — MIXED MAP (Hybrid)

**Characteristics: **Combines open central zones with dense cover on the flanks or around the objective. Requires teams to manage two types of combat simultaneously.

Why mixed maps are the most strategically demanding
On a mixed map, every role must fulfill its function in its zone but also have the ability to respond in the opposing zone. A Sniper on a mixed map who cannot adapt when enemies close the distance through the flanks loses 50% of their value. The most versatile Brawlers (Hybrids, some DPS and Supports) reach their peak performance on mixed maps.

**Examples: **Backyard Bowl, Center Stage, Pinball Dreams (maps with an open central zone and covered flanks).

orig_title: Analisis por Modo de Juego
title: Analysis by Game Mode
body:
Below, each main mode is analyzed with its mechanics, the real win condition, the most valuable roles, and the most common mistake that leads to defeat.

Gem GEM GRAB Zone control and resource management mode · 3v3

The central mine produces gems at regular intervals. The team that collects 10 gems and holds the countdown for 15 seconds wins. If the gem carrier dies, they drop all gems on the ground.

Real objective Dominate the center of the map to collect gems and protect the carrier when the countdown activates. Win condition Collect 10 gems by controlling the mine. Once the countdown activates: do NOT attack, protect the carrier, and deny the enemy access to the center.
Key roles Mid-range Controller or DPS to control the center. Support to keep the carrier alive while the countdown runs. Tank to open access to the mine. Most common mistake Continuing to play aggressively once the countdown is already active. The gem carrier entering the center with 10+ gems and exposing themselves.
Key concept: The gem is a passive resource, not a weapon
Gems deal no damage and grant no offensive bonuses. A team that collects 10 gems and switches from aggressive to defensive mode wins the mode — not the team with the most kills.
Ball BRAWL BALL Moving objective and collective pressure mode · 3v3

The first team to score 2 goals wins. The ball spawns in the center of the map and can be kicked with a normal attack or with the Super. Walls are destroyed when hit by the ball.

Real objective Create numerical superiority around the ball and project it toward the enemy goal along a clear trajectory. Win condition Create a path free of obstacles and enemy players so the ball enters the goal. Clear the way with the Super when there is an almost open route to the goal.
Key roles Tank to open space and absorb pressure near the goal. Fast DPS or a Brawler with good mobility to carry the ball. Support to maintain numerical advantage in your own half. Most common mistake Shooting the ball toward goal with enemies in the trajectory (a block is free for the opponent). Using the Super to attack instead of clearing the route toward goal.
Key concept: The ball is your team's Tank
The ball destroys walls and can be used as an area projectile. The best players don't just move the ball — they use it to deny space, destroy enemy cover, and create alternative routes toward goal.
Safe HEIST Assault and defense of a static objective mode · 3v3

Each team has a safe. The team that destroys the enemy safe first wins. If time runs out, the team that dealt the most damage to the enemy safe wins.

Real objective Maximize damage to the enemy safe while keeping your own safe alive long enough. Win condition Destroy the enemy safe before time runs out, or accumulate more damage on it. Manage your defense so that the damage margin always favors your team.
Key roles High DPS or Throwers to attack the safe from cover. Assassins to eliminate defenders and open the attack route. Brawlers with area-of-effect projectiles for continuous damage to the safe. Most common mistake Eliminating enemies instead of attacking the safe (kills don't award direct points). Defending passively without pressuring the enemy safe, losing by total damage dealt.
Key concept: The clock is your greatest enemy
Heist has a time limit. If the damage scoreboard is against you, you need to take risks to make a comeback. A team that only defends well but doesn't pressure the enemy safe will systematically lose by damage difference.
Star BOUNTY Kill management and objective value mode · 3v3

Each player has stars above their head that increase with each consecutive kill. Upon death, the enemy takes those stars. At the end of the timer, the team with the most accumulated stars wins.

Real objective Accumulate the highest number of stars possible by prioritizing the highest-value targets. Win condition Eliminating the enemy with the most stars maximizes the reward. Surviving increases your own value: a player with 5 stars is worth more alive than one with 1.
Key roles Snipers to eliminate high-value targets from a safe distance. Long-range DPS to accumulate kills with low risk. Support to keep allies with many stars alive. Most common mistake Dying unnecessarily while carrying many stars (donating stars to the enemy). Attacking the wrong target (the one with fewer stars) because they are easier to kill.
Key concept: Your value grows the longer you survive
In Bounty, conservative positioning is the dominant mechanic: the risk of dying not only costs you your stars, but also hands a massive bounty to the enemy. Strategic survival outperforms aggressive combat.
Zone HOT ZONE Zone control and territorial presence mode · 3v3

The map has one or more circular zones. Capturing a zone requires friendly players inside it and no enemies. The point bar advances while the zone is controlled.

Real objective Control the zones for as long as possible by expelling enemies from them or preventing their access. Win condition Occupy and retain zones with a live player inside, supported by the rest of the team from the outside. On single-zone maps: constant presence. On multi-zone maps: prioritize the most valuable zone.
Key roles Controllers to deny zones with area damage. Supports to keep the player inside the zone alive. Tanks to withstand damage inside the zone under pressure. Most common mistake Leaving the zone to chase an enemy outside it. Attempting to control all zones simultaneously without critical mass in any of them.
Key concept: One living player in the zone is worth more than three dead enemies outside it
Hot Zone is the mode where the mistake of prioritizing kills over objectives is most apparent. Killing all three enemies while your zone is empty earns you no points. One player surviving inside the zone under fire does more for the team than two teammates chasing kills outside.
Skull KNOCKOUT Elimination mode with no respawn · 3v3

No respawn: each player has only one life per round. The team that eliminates all enemies wins the round. The first to win two rounds wins the match. The zone shrinks over time.

Real objective Eliminate all enemies without losing any ally, managing positioning with extreme caution. Win condition Numerical superiority is absolute: 3v2 is almost always a win. Getting the first elimination wins the exchange. Force the enemy to die to the timer if the 3v3 is at a disadvantage.
Key roles Snipers to eliminate from a safe distance without overexposing. High burst DPS for quick kills before the enemy can respond. Brawlers with high survivability to avoid being the first to fall. Most common mistake Overextending to secure a kill that leaves the team in a 2v3. Not using the Super when there is a clean kill opportunity — in Knockout, there is no second chance.
Key concept: The first death defines the round
In Knockout, the first death almost always triggers the second, as the team with the advantage can play 3v2 in an encircling manner. The team that loses a player first enters defensive mode almost immediately.
Skull SHOWDOWN Solo and duo Battle Royale mode · 1v9 / 2v9

Ten players on a map with a shrinking zone. Power cubes increase damage and health. The scoring system rewards final placement, not just kills.

Real objective Survive as long as possible with the maximum number of Power cubes to compete effectively in the final encounters. Win condition Collect Power cubes without compromising your survival position. Avoid early combat unless it is safe and worthwhile.
Key roles Assassins with mobility to collect cubes and escape unfavorable confrontations. Throwers to attack from cover as the zone reduces available space. Brawlers with a high base health pool to endure the final encounters. Most common mistake Seeking aggressive combat in the early phase without enough Power cubes. Ignoring the zone: dying at the edge never earns trophies.
Key concept: Showdown is not won by killing — it is won by surviving
The Showdown trophy system rewards final placement. A player who finishes fourth without killing anyone can earn more trophies than one who eliminates five enemies but dies halfway through the match.

orig_title: Roles por Modo: Analisis Detallado
title: Roles by Mode: Detailed Analysis
body:
Below, each mode is analyzed with the three role coverage categories: essential (the team needs to cover this or it systematically loses), highly recommended (significantly improves the win condition), and dispensable or risky (works in very specific situations but penalizes in the majority).

GEM GRAB — Zone control and gem carrier
Essential Zone control (Controller or mid-range DPS) Sustain or support for the carrier Highly Recommended Active Support (Poco, Byron, Sandy) Tank to open access to the central mine Dispensable / Risky Triple Assassin (no zone control) Full Sniper (no presence in the center)
**Tactical note: **Zone coverage in the center is the primary need. Without at least one Brawler capable of holding and fighting in the mine area, the team never accumulates gems at a good rate.
BRAWL BALL — Route opening and physical superiority
Essential Physical presence near the ball (mid or short-range DPS) Ability to clear the path before shooting Highly Recommended Tank to withstand pressure at the own goal Brawler with area Super to clear opponents in the shooting zone Dispensable / Risky Full Sniper (unable to get close to the ball) Triple Support (insufficient damage to clear opponents)
**Tactical note: **Brawl Ball rewards physicality: the team that can move the ball safely and clear the path toward the goal with their Super wins the decisive exchanges. Movement speed and short-to-mid range damage are the most valuable attributes.
BOUNTY — Precision, positioning and target priority
Essential Long-range damage with low risk profile (Sniper or long-range DPS) Highly Recommended Support to keep the ally with the most stars alive Second Sniper or range DPS for cross pressure Dispensable / Risky Tank on open map (dies before reaching range) Aggressive Assassin (their risk profile penalizes in a mode where dying is very costly)
**Tactical note: **Bounty is the mode where individual risk profile penalizes the team the most. Every own death is a star gift to the opponent. The optimal composition minimizes individual exposure: a team that never dies wins even without aggressive kills.
HOT ZONE — Sustained presence and zone denial
Essential Brawler capable of surviving inside the zone under pressure (Tank or high-health brawler) Highly Recommended Controller to deny the zone with area damage from the outside Support to keep the player inside the zone alive Dispensable / Risky Triple Assassin (die trying to hold the zone) Full Sniper (cannot be inside the zone safely)
**Tactical note: **Hot Zone is the mode where physical presence inside the circle is worth more than any other consideration. A mediocre Tank inside the zone under a Support's cover is worth more than three S-tier Snipers shooting from outside with no one on the objective.
HEIST — Sustained damage on objective and route opening
Essential High sustained DPS on the safe (Thrower or DPS with area projectile) Ability to clear defenders on the access route Highly Recommended High-mobility Brawler to pressure from two flanks Thrower to attack from cover without exposing themselves Dispensable / Risky Triple Support (insufficient damage on the safe) Full Tank (high sustain but insufficient damage over time)
**Tactical note: **Heist has the clock working against you. The optimal composition maximizes damage per second to the safe, not team survival. A team that plays to survive in Heist instead of systematically attacking the safe loses by damage difference even if nobody dies.
KNOCKOUT — Burst, survival and pressure without respawning
Essential Burst capability to secure the first elimination before the full exchange Highly Recommended High-survival Brawler to avoid being the first to fall Sniper or long-range DPS to eliminate from a safe position Dispensable / Risky Pure Support (no own kills, relies on allies not dying) Slow Thrower on open map (no cover, no value)
**Tactical note: **In Knockout, the Support loses much of its value because there is no respawn to manage: if an ally dies, no Support can retroactively prevent it. The mode rewards burst and individual evasion more than sustained healing.
SHOWDOWN — Individual survival and risk management
Profiles that work well High mobility to collect boxes and avoid dangerous areas. High base health to endure the final encounters with power-ups. Throwers: great value in the final phase when the zone is reduced. Profiles that penalize Pure Supports: without fixed allies to protect, their value drops drastically. Snipers on closed Showdown maps: without line of sight their damage is null.
**Tactical note: **Showdown is the only mode where the Support role loses almost all its value. The logic of the mode is individual: each player manages their own life and risk. The concept of role coverage does not apply in the same way as in 3v3 modes.

orig_title: Los Arquetipos de Composicion
title: The Composition Archetypes
body:
From the combination of the three previous axes, six composition archetypes emerge that cover the vast majority of situations in Brawl Stars. Each archetype has a name, an internal logic, examples of trios, and their win and loss conditions.

ARCHETYPE 1 — Poke + Tank (The classic 3v3 formation)
Role structure 1 Tank opens space and absorbs damage on the front line. 1-2 mid-to-long range DPS deal damage from behind. Optional: 1 Support or Controller for zone control. Win condition The Tank generates contact pressure that forces the opponent to fall back. The DPS punishes exposed positions while the Tank occupies space. The team advances in a coordinated manner until controlling the objective. Main vulnerability Vulnerable to full-range compositions that kill the Tank before it gets in range. Loses against Throwers on closed maps that attack the Tank from cover. Without a Support, the Tank falls quickly if the opponent focuses it down coordinately.
Optimal modes Gem Grab, Hot Zone, Brawl Ball Preferred map types Lane and mixed maps — the Tank needs cover to advance

Trio examples:

Bull Front-line Tank Brock Long-range DPS Poco Healing Support
Bull opens the central lane and forces the opponent to fall back. Brock punishes any exposed position from behind. Poco keeps Bull alive long enough to reach combat range.
Frank Tank with control super Colt Long-range DPS Emz Zone Controller
Frank initiates with his super to stun them and create an opening. Colt exploits the exposed position of stunned opponents. Emz denies the retreat with her slow field.
ARCHETYPE 2 — Full Aggro (The maximum pressure formation)
Role structure 2-3 short-range or high-mobility Brawlers. No Support or Thrower. The team bets on collective burst. Emphasis on getting in, eliminating, and getting out before taking sustained damage. Win condition The team seeks the quick elimination of the first opponent to create a 3v2 advantage. In a 3v2 situation, the team surrounds and eliminates the second opponent before the third can react. The kill chain closes before the opponent can respond. Main vulnerability Loses against compositions with an active Support that interrupts the kill chain. Loses in modes where kills don't generate a direct advantage (Hot Zone, ball-focused Brawl Ball). Very map-dependent: without cover to approach, Assassins can't get in.
Optimal modes Knockout, Bounty (with a conservative profile), Brawl Ball (with Tanks instead of Assassins) Preferred map types Closed maps with bushes — allow for concealed approach

Trio examples:

Mortis Main Assassin Leon Flanking Assassin Fang Cleanup DPS-Assassin
Mortis and Leon enter from different angles to split the opponent's attention. Fang closes the combo with his area super when opponents are weakened by the first hits.
Edgar Aggressive Assassin Bibi Short-range DPS El Primo Hybrid Tank-Assassin
All three enter together forming a wall of short-range pressure. The goal is not to maneuver but to overwhelm the opponent with simultaneous damage from three fronts.
ARCHETYPE 3 — Poke + Thrower (Cover control)
Role structure 1-2 Throwers to attack from behind walls. 1 mid-range DPS or Controller to deny access. No front-line Tank: the map's cover acts as a shield. Win condition Throwers generate constant damage on covered positions where the opponent takes refuge. The DPS denies the advance of opponents trying to get close to silence the Throwers. The team wins through attrition: the opponent has no safe angle to position themselves. Main vulnerability Loses completely on open maps with no walls to use as a shield. Vulnerable to high-mobility Assassins that can flank and reach the Throwers. Loses in Brawl Ball and modes where you need to move with the objective.
Optimal modes Heist, Gem Grab on closed maps, Hot Zone with covered zones Preferred map types Closed lane maps with dense cover — essential for this archetype

Trio examples:

Barley Main Thrower Dynamike Flanking Thrower Emz Access Controller
Barley and Dynamike cover the center of the map from opposite positions, creating crossfire damage zones where the opponent has no safe angle. Emz protects the flanks with her slow field to prevent Assassins from reaching the Throwers.
ARCHETYPE 4 — Protect the Carry (The star player's shield)
Role structure 1 high-damage or technically skilled Brawler (the carry). 1-2 Brawlers dedicated to keeping the carry alive and creating space for them. The team orbits around the carry: their decisions protect and empower them. Win condition The carry accumulates kills, gems, or stars while the team absorbs damage and creates the conditions. The team identifies when the carry needs space and when they need healing. The win condition is that the carry shoulders the offensive load while the team manages collective survival. Main vulnerability If the carry dies, the team loses all offensive capability: there is no plan B. Requires implicit coordination: in random matches without communication, the team may not understand they need to protect the carry. Vulnerable to compositions that focus the carry with Assassins or cross pressure.
Optimal modes Gem Grab (carry = gem holder), Bounty (carry = player with most stars), Heist Preferred map types Any map archetype depending on the chosen carry

Trio examples:

Piper Long-range Carry Sandy Support-Controller Rosa Protective Tank
Piper needs open space and to avoid being reached in order to maximize her damage. Sandy creates fog zones to protect her and Rosa absorbs any Assassin that tries to flank. The entire team exists so that Piper can shoot freely.
ARCHETYPE 5 — Stall / Control (The attrition and denial formation)
Role structure 2-3 Brawlers with zone control, healing, or high survivability. No aggressive carry. The team doesn't look for kills — it looks to deny space. The objective is to prevent the opponent from operating in the key zones of the map. Win condition The team controls the center or objective zone with sustained presence. The opponent cannot advance without taking continuous damage or being slowed. The match is won by time: the team maintains control long enough to trigger the countdown or accumulate points. Main vulnerability Loses in time-limited modes where the opponent can afford to be passive (Heist with the clock). Vulnerable to compositions with high burst that can clear the control before the team can react. Loses in Bounty if the opponent plays more conservatively and accumulates stars without exposing themselves.
Optimal modes Gem Grab, Hot Zone, long-duration zone modes Preferred map types Lane maps with lateral cover — allows control without full exposure

Trio examples:

Poco Healing Support Emz Zone Controller Jacky Control Tank
Poco passively heals the entire team while Emz slows down opponents trying to approach the objective. Jacky absorbs direct damage and keeps the space controlled. The team doesn't need to kill anyone: it just needs the opponent to be unable to operate in the zone.
ARCHETYPE 6 — Counterpush (The counterattack formation)
Role structure Mix of Brawlers with high health and medium mobility. The team holds in defense, accumulates supers, and counterattacks with a resource advantage. There is no carry or fixed role: everyone rotates according to the state of the map. Win condition The team absorbs the opponent's pressure, accumulates all players' supers, and activates the coordinated counterattack. With three supers charged, the team enters simultaneous aggressive mode and cleans out the opponent in a favorable exchange. After the counterattack, the team quickly executes the objective while the opponent is dead or retreating. Main vulnerability Requires knowing when the right moment for the counterattack is: too early = not enough supers. Too late = the opponent has already executed their objective. In very short-time modes, the opponent can win before the team accumulates the resources for the counter. Difficult to execute in random matches without implicit coordination.
Optimal modes Brawl Ball, Gem Grab, Knockout Preferred map types Mixed and lane maps — allow absorbing and then rotating

Trio examples:

Ash Tank with fury super Byron Support with inversion super Buzz Assassin with hook super
Ash withstands the first opponent onslaught and charges his fury. Byron keeps the team alive and can reverse his healing onto the opponent at the key moment. Buzz waits for the right super moment to leap onto the most exposed opponent and initiate the counterattack.

orig_title: Pares de alta sinergia: analisis mecanico
title: High synergy pairs: mechanical analysis
body:
The following pairs represent some of the strongest synergies in the game. Each case is analyzed based on why they work at a mechanical level, not just intuitively.

Sandy + Leon Sandy · Leon │ Enablement — Cross invisibility
Why it works mechanically Sandy puts rivals to sleep with his super and creates a fog area that makes allies inside invisible. Leon activates his individual invisibility to approach the already-sleeping target with no risk of being detected or intercepted. The rival cannot react: they are asleep, in the fog, and with an Assassin on top of them. Activation condition Sandy's super must connect before Leon enters. If Leon enters before Sandy's super lands, the rival can see Leon approaching and prepare. The order is critical: Sandy first, Leon after.
**When the synergy breaks: **If the rival has a Brawler with area control that hits the sleeping targets (such as Barley or Sprout), the fog does not protect Leon because the area damage wakes up the sleeping rivals before Leon arrives.
Poco + any Tank Poco · Bull / Jacky / Frank / Rosa │ Amplification — Front line sustain
Why it works mechanically Poco passively heals all allies in range with each attack and with his super. The Tank can advance into combat range absorbing shots because Poco's healing compensates for the damage taken during the advance. Without Poco, the Tank arrives at combat range with 40-50% health. With Poco, they arrive with 80-90%. Activation condition Poco needs to be close enough to the Tank for the healing to reach them, but not so close as to be hit by the rivals the Tank is pressuring. The optimal distance is Poco's healing radius minus one step: staying just behind the Tank.
**When the synergy breaks: **If the rival has Assassins that flank Poco while the Tank advances, Poco dies and the Tank loses their sustain at the most critical moment. Poco's protection is the weakness of this pair.
Mortis + Thrower (Barley / Dynamike) Mortis · Barley / Dynamike / Tick │ Mutual coverage — Classic anti-weakness
Why it works mechanically Throwers have zero mobility and are extremely vulnerable to Assassins that flank them. Mortis intercepts exactly that profile: he moves fast, operates at close range, and can chase and eliminate any Assassin before they reach the Thrower. The Thrower, in turn, denies the zones where Mortis cannot operate (behind walls), completing the map coverage. Activation condition Mortis must be positioned between the Thrower and the rival's approach flanks. If Mortis enters aggressive mode and moves away from the Thrower, the pair breaks: the Thrower becomes exposed and the rival eliminates them without resistance.
**When the synergy breaks: **If the rival brings two Assassins, Mortis can only intercept one. The Thrower remains exposed to the second. This pair needs a third Brawler with zone control to cover the second flank.
Max + Assassin (any) Max · Leon / Mortis / Fang / Edgar │ Amplification — Entry and exit speed
Why it works mechanically Max grants a movement speed bonus to all allies with her super. For an Assassin, speed is the difference between completing the elimination combo and being left exposed on the way back. With Max's super active, the Assassin enters 15-20% faster (the rival has less time to react), deals their damage, and exits range before receiving the rival team's full response. Activation condition The optimal activation moment for Max's super is just before the Assassin enters: the Assassin should start their approach when they see the super activate, not before. If the Assassin enters before the super, the speed advantage only applies to the exit, not the entry.
**When the synergy breaks: **Max has low health and can be focused easily. If the rival eliminates Max before she can use the super, the Assassin loses their advantage and is left with their standard entry profile, which is more predictable.
Byron + high-damage DPS Byron · Brock / Belle / Colt / 8-Bit │ Amplification — Healing inversion and increased damage
Why it works mechanically Byron can both heal allies and poison rivals with the same projectile depending on who the target is. His super injects all allies in the area with an effect that heals by a percentage of health. In combination with a high-damage DPS like Brock, Byron allows the DPS to absorb more punishment before needing to retreat, extending the window in which the DPS can operate at maximum damage position. Activation condition The synergy is most effective when the DPS can hold their firing position for extended periods, meaning on open maps or lane maps with lateral cover. If the map forces the DPS to retreat constantly, Byron's healing cannot compensate for the damage received.
**When the synergy breaks: **If the rival has Assassins that quickly eliminate the DPS, Byron cannot heal fast enough. The synergy requires sustained combat, not burst exchanges.

orig_title: Los tres casos de rol en equipo random
title: The three cases of random team role
body:
By applying the previous process, your Brawler inevitably falls into one of three cases. Each case has different implications on how you should behave during the match.

CASE A: Your role fits the team Your Brawler fills exactly a gap that the team needs. The team has tactical coherence without any adjustments needed. Your behavior can follow the standard for your role: do what your Brawler does well without needing to compensate for anything. How to act: Play your role orthodoxly. Do not improvise or try to cover more than what falls to you. Most mistakes in this case come from overstepping the role and leaving your own gap exposed. CASE B: Your role overlaps with an ally's Two or three Brawlers on the team cover the same role. The team has an excess in one function and a gap in another. You need to decide whether to play your primary role (and accept the overlap) or adapt your behavior toward the missing role. How to act: Identify the critical gap that nobody covers. If your Brawler has a secondary role that can cover it, activate that secondary role. If not, play your primary role but communicate through positioning that the team has a weakness. CASE C: Your role is not needed by the team or the map Your Brawler is out of place in this context. The mode does not require your role, the map nullifies it, or the team already covers it in excess. This is the hardest case because there is no adjustment that resolves the fundamental mismatch. How to act: Minimize the damage: play conservatively so you are not an active burden. Identify the one thing your Brawler can do well in this context, even if only partially. Surviving and not losing resources is more valuable than trying to play aggressively out of context.

orig_title: Los cinco conceptos posicionales fundamentales
title: The five fundamental positional concepts
body:
These five concepts define how a team moves and positions itself effectively. They apply to any mode and map archetype.

Vision Control Controlling that the opponent cannot see your position while you can see theirs. In Brawl Stars, vision is gained through bush presence and lost when the opponent has Brawlers in elevated positions or their own bushes. Vision control is not just about invisibility: it is about information. Knowing where the opponent is before they know where you are is an enormous tactical advantage. **Example: **A Leon in a bush on the dominant flank has vision control: the opponent does not know whether he is there or not, which forces them to play more conservatively. If the opponent shoots into the bush preemptively, Leon loses vision control even if he is still alive.
Lane Pressure The ability of a Brawler to force the opponent to fall back or refrain from advancing in a given lane through the threat of damage. Lane pressure does not require killing the opponent: it requires the opponent to perceive that advancing will cost more than it will gain. A Sniper with vision of the central lane generates lane pressure without even firing at anyone. **Example: **Piper positioned at the back of the map with a line of sight to the center generates lane pressure on any opponent who attempts to advance along that line. If the opponent advances, they receive the full damage from Piper's charged attack. If they do not advance, Piper's team controls the tempo.
Shooting Angle The position from which your Brawler can hit the opponent for maximum damage while minimizing damage received. A favorable shooting angle is one where your projectile reaches the opponent before their projectile reaches you, or where the cover between you protects you but not them. Finding and maintaining the optimal shooting angle is the most underutilized positional skill. **Example: **Brock on the far right of the map has a lateral shooting angle over the center: his projectiles reach the opponent at the objective from an angle that the lane walls cannot block. If Brock moves to the center, those same walls block his own projectiles.
Rotation The deliberate movement of a Brawler from one area of the map to another in order to cover a gap, reinforce a flank under pressure, or capitalize on a numerical advantage elsewhere on the map. Rotation is the difference between a team that reacts and a team that anticipates. Rotating too late means arriving after the damage is already done; rotating too early means leaving your own area undefended. **Example: **The Tank has cleared the right flank and the opponent has no presence there. The center DPS rotates to the right flank to create cross pressure: the opponent now has to defend two points with two Brawlers while the allied team pushes from two angles.
Conceding Ground with Purpose Deliberately falling back to trade space for other advantages: time to recharge the super, enough distance to heal, or a more favorable position for the counterattack. Conceding ground is not losing: it is a tactical decision when the cost of holding a position outweighs the benefit of having it. **Example: **The gem carrier in Gem Grab with the countdown active retreats from the center to their own base, ceding the center to the opponent. This deliberate concession of ground shifts the opponent's attack cost: to stop the countdown, the opponent must advance toward the enemy base, under allied fire, while the countdown keeps running.

orig_title: El momento optimo de la super segun el modo
title: The optimal moment to use the super according to the game mode
body:
The super does not have a universal moment of use: the game mode context defines which window of maximum impact applies. The following table establishes the criterion for each mode:

Mode Key moment Why that moment and not another
Gem Grab When the enemy countdown is active Interrupting the enemy's active countdown has maximum value: you prevent them from winning and force them to lose countdown time. Using the super to attack when no countdown is active has far less impact.
Brawl Ball Before a shot on goal to clear enemies A super that clears the path to the goal is worth a potential goal. A super used to get a kill in your own half is worth a kill with no direct impact on the score.
Heist To clear defenders on the route to the safe Every second a defender stays alive near the safe is damage your team cannot deal. The super that eliminates the main defender opens the route for DPS brawlers to deal free damage to the safe.
Bounty To eliminate the enemy with the most stars The impact of the super is measured in stars: a super that kills the enemy with 6 stars is worth 6 times more than one that kills the enemy with 1 star. Always prioritize the highest-value target.
Hot Zone To clear the zone when the enemy occupies it If the enemy is in the zone and the bar is advancing, a super that expels them stops the counter and can reverse it. It is the highest-impact use per second of any mode.
Knockout As soon as a kill is guaranteed In Knockout there is no respawn. Saving the super waiting for the perfect moment can cost you the round if the enemy eliminates you first. The threshold for a "good enough moment" is lower than in other modes.
Showdown In the final phase with a reduced zone In the early phase, the super can save you but its use is less decisive because many players are still alive. In the final phase, a well-used super can eliminate the last enemy and win the match.

orig_title: Como leer un matchup en cinco segundos
title: How to read a matchup in five seconds
body:
Before each match you can see the opponent's Brawlers. The following process allows you to evaluate your critical matchups quickly:

Question What it determines
1 Who has more range on this map? If the opponent outranges you AND the map is open, you are at a structural range disadvantage. If the map is closed, the opponent's range is neutralized and the matchup rebalances.
2 Can the opponent reach me before I eliminate them? Calculate whether your total DPS can eliminate the opponent before they close into your range (for Assassins) or before their accumulated DPS takes you out of the fight (for sustained duels).
3 Does any of their Supers directly counter me? Identify whether the opponent's Super nullifies your main mechanic. Sandy's sleep ruins Assassins. Frank's stun neutralizes any DPS Brawler that needs to move to deal damage.
4 In which zone of the map will the duel take place? The matchup is not abstract: it happens at a specific position on the map. A matchup you lose in open ground can become even if the duel occurs in the cover zone where your Brawler operates best.
5 Do I have a net advantage or disadvantage? Add up the axes: if you win 3 out of 4, you have the advantage. If you lose 3 out of 4, you are at a disadvantage. If it is 2-2, the matchup is decided by execution, not mechanics. This is the only case where individual skill makes the difference.

orig_title: Los cinco factores que hacen a un Brawler meta
title: The five factors that make a Brawler meta
body:
When a Brawler rises to the meta, there is always a mechanical reason behind it. Identifying that reason allows you to predict whether the Brawler's meta position is solid or fragile, and anticipate how long it will last.

Factor 1 — Mode Versatility A Brawler that performs well in three or more game modes has a more stable meta value than one specialized in just one. Even if it is not the best in any single mode, its consistency across modes gives it an extremely high pick value in environments where several modes are played consecutively. **Practical example: **Max is frequently meta not because she dominates in Gem Grab or Bounty individually, but because her utility (speed for allies, solid DPS, healing with Super) provides value in virtually any mode. Her winrate is consistently good in six of the seven modes.
Factor 2 — Dominance in Active Maps When the active map rotation favors a specific archetype (many closed maps, many lane maps, many open maps), Brawlers of that archetype increase in value even if their stats have not changed. This is the most volatile meta factor: it changes every rotation. **Practical example: **Barley and Dynamike rise and fall from the meta following exactly the proportion of closed maps in rotation. When Heist and Gem Grab feature maps with high wall density, Throwers are meta. When the maps open up, they disappear from the meta even though Supercell has not touched their stats.
Factor 3 — Absence of Accessible Counterpick A Brawler can be meta simply because there is no easy counterpick to obtain or to play. If the Brawler that should counter it is not available at lower ranks or requires a lot of skill to be effective, the Brawler without a counterpick dominates by default. **Practical example: **In ranks where Leon is very common, his natural counterpicks (Brawlers with invisibility detection or area damage that reveals him) are not always available or easy to play. Leon dominates those ranks not because he is the best Brawler mechanically, but because his weaknesses are not represented.
Factor 4 — Synergy with the Dominant Meta Brawler When a Brawler enters the meta due to its stats or the maps, it brings along the Brawlers that synergize best with it. Synergy with the dominant Brawler of the moment is one of the fastest ways to rise to the meta without receiving a direct buff. **Practical example: **When Sandy increases in value (typically in rotations with a lot of Gem Grab and Hot Zone), Leon rises with him almost automatically. Their cross-invisibility synergy makes the two together much more dangerous than each one separately, and Sandy's meta pulls Leon's meta along with it.
Factor 5 — Learning Curve vs. Performance at a Specific Rank The meta is not universal: it is rank-specific. A Brawler with a high learning curve (Leon, Mortis, Piper) has low winrates at beginner ranks not because it is bad but because its mechanics require mastery to express their true value. At higher ranks, those same Brawlers can be absolutely dominant. **Practical example: **Mortis has very disparate winrates depending on the rank. At lower ranks, players do not know how to manage the entry and exit window and he dies constantly. At higher ranks, played correctly, he is one of the most difficult Assassins to neutralize. His position in the meta at low and high ranks is the opposite.

orig_title: Arquetipos y Roles de Brawlers
title: Brawler Archetypes and Roles
body: The first step to thinking strategically in Brawl Stars is to stop seeing Brawlers as a collection of statistics and start seeing them as functional roles. A role defines what a Brawler does within a team, not how much damage it deals or how much health it can withstand.

orig_title: Roles Secundarios y Flexibilidad
title: Secondary Roles and Flexibility
body:
Many Brawlers have a secondary role that determines how they should be used in certain contexts. Understanding this avoids one of the most common mistakes: assuming a Brawler should always behave the same way.

Brawler Primary Role Secondary Role When the secondary role activates
Crow Assassin Controller When poisoning multiple enemies in a closed area
Max Support Damage Dealer On open maps with no allies to boost
Sandy Support Controller When the Super creates a fog zone over a key objective
Brock Sniper Thrower When throwing rockets into covered areas with his Gadget
Chester Damage Dealer Assassin When his Super enables a quick elimination combo

orig_title: Lectura Táctica Rápida de un Mapa
title: Quick Tactical Map Reading
body:
Before the match begins you have a few seconds to analyze the map. This three-step process lets you extract the essential tactical information in under ten seconds:

Step Question What it determines
① Where is there cover and where is there not? Locate the areas where your Brawler can operate safely and the areas where it will be exposed. If your Brawler needs cover to be effective and the map does not provide it, you need to switch Brawlers or change your playstyle.
② How much space is there between teams? Estimate the average combat distance. Does the map force combat at short, medium, or long range? This defines whether your range is a structural advantage or disadvantage.
③ Where is the objective and how is it accessed? Identify the routes to the objective and whether they are open or covered. This determines the value of Throwers (attack from cover), Tanks (open up exposed routes), and Assassins (use alternative routes).

orig_title: Matriz Comparativa de Modos
title: Comparative Mode Matrix
body:
The following table compares all modes across the most relevant strategic dimensions:

Mode Priority Kills matter Position matters Support useful Decisive tempo Pace
Gem Grab Objective Medium High Yes Active counter Medium
Brawl Ball Ball/Goal Medium High Yes Shot on goal Fast
Heist Safe damage Medium Medium Little Final clock Fast
Bounty Stars High High Yes High-value kill Slow

Knockout Elimination Critical High Little First kill Variable
