orig_title: Matriz Global de Cobertura de Roles por Modo
title: Global Role Coverage Matrix by Mode
body:
The following matrix condenses all the information from the section into a quick reference. Use it before selecting your Brawler to verify whether your choice fills a real gap in the team.

Role Gem Grab Brawl Ball Bounty Knockout Hot Zone / Heist
Tank Useful Essential Dispensable Useful Essential
Sniper Useful Dispensable Essential Highly recommended Situational
Mid DPS Essential Essential Highly recommended Essential Highly recommended
Assassin Situational Highly recommended Dispensable Useful Highly recommended
Support Essential Highly recommended Highly recommended Dispensable Essential
Thrower Useful Dispensable Situational Dispensable Essential
Controller Essential Useful Useful Situational Essential
Essential — the team needs it Highly recommended — improves the win condition Useful — contributes in this mode Dispensable or Risky — avoid unless synergy exists

orig_title: Como elegir el arquetipo segun modo y mapa
title: How to choose the archetype according to mode and map
body:
The archetype choice is not free: it is imposed by the mode and the map. The following two-step process allows you to identify which archetype is optimal before selecting your Brawler:

Step 1: Identify the map archetype If the map is OPEN: discard Thrower + Poke and Full Aggro without cover. Viable archetypes are Poke + Tank, Protect the Carry and Stall with long range. If the map is CLOSED: Throwers are viable. Full Aggro with Assassins can work. Discard pure long-range compositions. If the map is LANE-BASED: Poke + Tank and Stall are the most natural archetypes. Counterpush works well with lanes defined as rotation lines. If the map is MIXED: all archetypes are viable depending on how you distribute roles across the different zones of the map.
Step 2: Filter by game mode GEM GRAB: Poke + Tank or Stall are the most consistent. Protect the Carry works with the gem carrier as the carry. BRAWL BALL: Full Aggro with Tanks, Counterpush. Discard Thrower + Poke (they cannot move the ball). HEIST: Thrower + Poke or Full Aggro depending on the map. Discard pure Stall (not enough damage on the safe). BOUNTY: Protect the Carry or conservative Poke + Tank. Discard aggressive Full Aggro (the risk profile penalizes). HOT ZONE: Stall / Control or Poke + Tank with Tank in the zone. Discard Assassin Full Aggro. KNOCKOUT: Full Aggro or Poke + Tank with burst. Discard pure Stall (without respawn, stalling is useless).

orig_title: Trios de alta sinergia: el equipo como sistema
title: High-synergy trios: the team as a system
body:
A high-synergy trio is not just three good overlapping pairs: it is a team where all three Brawlers enhance each other in a way that losing any one of them destroys the logic of the whole setup. These are some of the most coherent trios in the current game:

Trio Why it works as a system Breaking condition
Sandy + Leon + Crow Triple invisible control. Sandy creates a fog zone. Leon enters and eliminates. Crow poisons anyone who tries to escape or heal. The opponent has no safe angle: if they push forward, fog. If they retreat, poison. If they stay, Leon. If the opponent has a Brawler with area damage that clears the fog (Barley, Sprout), the foundation of the synergy disappears.
Poco + Bull + Brock Bull opens up frontline space. Poco keeps him alive during the push. Brock punishes the positions that Bull has forced enemies out of cover into. All three have complementary roles and none of them depend on the others doing anything outside their normal function. If Poco is eliminated early, Bull reaches range with very low health and Brock is left exposed with no flank protection.
Max + Mortis + Thrower Max speeds up Mortis so he intercepts enemy Assassins faster. The Thrower operates behind walls without exposure. Max also speeds up the Thrower if they need to reposition. All three cover different ranges with no overlap. If the opponent brings two Assassins, Mortis cannot cover both flanks even with Max's speed boost. The Thrower is left exposed.
Byron + Brock + Rosa Rosa tanks close-range combat with her invulnerable shield. Brock punishes from long range. Byron keeps both of them operational with passive healing and amplifies their damage with his Super. The opponent has to choose between pressuring Rosa (and taking Brock's damage) or eliminating Brock (and Rosa activates the shield). If the opponent focuses and eliminates Byron first, Rosa and Brock lose their sustain and Byron's offensive combo. Without Byron, the trio loses its glue.
Sprout + Emz + Jacky Sprout blocks routes with his vine wall Super. Emz slows down anyone who tries to go around her. Jacky pulls in those who manage to get close. The trio turns any zone into a space where the opponent cannot move freely. If the opponent has Throwers (Barley, Dynamike), they can attack all three from behind walls, ignoring the trio's zone control.

orig_title: Escenarios resueltos: el proceso en accion
title: Solved Scenarios: The Process in Action
body:
The following scenarios apply the five-question process to specific in-game situations. Each one shows how the same logic produces different conclusions depending on the context.

Scenario 1 — The Support Who Isn't Healing Gem Grab │ Lane map with a contested center
Your team Ally 1: Bull (aggressive Tank) Ally 2: Brock (long-range Sniper) You: Poco (Support healer) Inferred role The team has a frontline opener (Bull) and long-range damage (Brock). What's missing is center control and sustain for the gem carrier. You are the glue: your role is to keep whoever is collecting gems alive and to position yourself in the center so your healing reaches both Bull and the carrier. Your priorities Position yourself in the center behind Bull, not in the backline with Brock. Prioritize healing the ally carrying the most gems. Do not enter direct combat range: your value lies in your healing radius, not your damage. Save your Super for the moment the gem countdown activates.
**Most common mistake in this situation: **Staying with Brock in the backline. Poco healing from the rear can't reach Bull on the frontline, and the Tank falls without sustain before he can create space for gem collection.
Scenario 2 — The Assassin Who Can't Get In Bounty │ Open map with no bushes or cover
Your team Ally 1: Piper (Sniper) Ally 2: Colt (long-range DPS) You: Mortis (Assassin) Inferred role Case C: your Brawler is out of place. The open map eliminates your ability to approach from cover, and the mode (Bounty) severely punishes dying — which is exactly the risk you take when attempting to close in at short range. The team doesn't need an Assassin in this context: it would need a third Sniper or DPS. Your priorities Play on the map's periphery using whatever minimal cover is available. Do not attempt to close in at short range: the penalty for dying in Bounty is too high. Use Mortis's dash to dodge shots and stay alive, not to approach enemies. Your goal is to not die and not donate stars to the enemy, not to get kills.
**Most common mistake in this situation: **Trying to play Mortis in his orthodox style — dive in, eliminate, and escape — on an open Bounty map. Every entry attempt, successful or not, carries an enormous cost: if you die, you donate stars. If you go in and get a kill but take heavy damage, your star value rises and you become a priority target.
Scenario 3 — The Role Overlap Hot Zone │ Mixed map with an open central zone
Your team Ally 1: Frank (slow Tank) Ally 2: Jacky (control Tank) You: Emz (Controller) Inferred role Case B: the team has two Tanks but neither can sustain a presence inside the zone without external support. Emz has zone control but her moderate health pool makes her vulnerable inside the circle. The overlap is Frank-Jacky (double Tank). The gap is someone who can hold the zone with sustain. Emz must adapt her behavior: instead of denying the zone from the outside, act as a third body inside the zone, using her slow field to deny enemy access while Frank and Jacky absorb the damage. Your priorities Position yourself on the edge of the zone, not outside it. Use Emz's slow field to deny enemies access to the zone. Let Frank and Jacky absorb direct damage while you deny the outer radius. If Frank or Jacky fall, retreat outside the zone and wait for them to return before re-entering.
**Most common mistake in this situation: **Staying on the outside and playing Emz as a pure flank Controller while the zone is left with only two Tanks and no support. The enemy can send in an Assassin and quickly clear the zone.
Scenario 4 — The Improvised Carry Brawl Ball │ Closed map with side lanes
Your team Ally 1: Barley (Thrower) Ally 2: Byron (Support) You: Bibi (short-range DPS) Inferred role The team has long-range coverage (Barley) and sustain (Byron), but no one who can move the ball effectively in open space. Bibi is the only Brawler on the team with a physical presence near the ball and the ability to fight in the space between goals. Your role means being the ball carry: you are the one who must collect, move, and shoot the ball, while Barley denies the area around the enemy goal and Byron keeps you alive. Your priorities Always position yourself near the ball, not on the flanks. Use Bibi's bubble to push enemies out of the shooting lane before striking the ball. Communicate your position through movement: advancing toward the ball is the signal for Byron to heal you and Barley to cover the area ahead. Save your Super for the moment you have a clean angle on goal.
**Most common mistake in this situation: **Playing Bibi as pure DPS hunting kills on the flanks while the ball goes uncontrolled. A ball without a carrier doesn't score goals: every second Bibi spends away from the ball is a second the team cannot advance.

orig_title: Posicionamiento por rol
title: Positioning by role
body:
Each role has an optimal position range relative to the frontline. Operating outside that range —too far forward or too far back— destroys the Brawler's value regardless of their stats.

Role Optimal position Why there and not further forward Why there and not further back
Tank Front line, maximum possible contact Further forward they have no support. They lose their allies' cover and die without having created space. Further back they generate no pressure. The opponent doesn't need to fall back and the team doesn't advance on the objective.
Sniper Back of the map, maximum line of sight to the objective Further forward they enter the opponent's response range before their damage has generated enough of an advantage. Further back they lose line of sight to the objective and their projectiles get blocked by the map's geometry.
Mid DPS Second line, behind the Tank or cover Further forward they become the opponent's priority target without the Tank's health to absorb the initial damage. Further back their damage doesn't reach the frontline and the Tank fights alone without damage support.
Assassin Flank or nearby cover, ready to engage Engaging too early before having the right angle reveals their position and the opponent can prepare. Further back they miss the window of opportunity: the target moves, heals, or receives support before you arrive.
Support Behind the DPS, within healing range of the Tank Further forward they are easily eliminated: without high health, the Support cannot absorb front line damage. Further back their healing doesn't reach the Tank or the DPS who need it at the critical moment.
Thrower Behind a wall with an angle to the objective or the opponent Further forward they lose the wall's cover: their arcing projectiles have no advantage over the opponent's direct shots. Further back their projectile arc doesn't reach the objective or the combat area where they need to deal damage.
Controller Center or edge of the objective, highest traffic area Further forward in an open position their damage zone is exposed and the opponent simply goes around them. Further back their control field doesn't reach the objective and the opponent can operate within it without taking damage.

orig_title: El Gadget: precision y timing sobre potencia
title: The Gadget: precision and timing over power
body:
The gadget is a resource with limited uses (generally 3) that does not recharge during the match. Unlike the super, the gadget does not charge: you have exactly the uses you start with, and once they run out, they run out. This makes it the resource that penalizes waste the most.

THE GADGET Limited-use ability (2-3 charges). Does not recharge during the match. Activates a unique secondary effect of the Brawler.
When to save it When the gadget covers a situation you could resolve just as well without it: if you can dodge a projectile by moving, don't use the invulnerability gadget. During the early phase of the match unless the situation is life or death: gadgets in the final phase are worth more because opponents are also more weakened. In response to threats the opponent deliberately generates to force your gadget: some opponents press intentionally to make you use the gadget before the real play. When a more critical situation is anticipated in the next 30 seconds. When to use it To complete a kill that you cannot close without the gadget: the gadget that turns a failed attempt into a guaranteed kill has a clear return. To escape a situation where death is almost certain without it: one gadget use that saves your life is worth exactly the difference between living and dying. To activate the critical synergy with your ally: if the gadget enables a play that is impossible without it (Rosa's invulnerability gadget that allows an ally to charge their super without risk), the moment of use is dictated by that synergy. At the decisive moment of the mode: the last minute of Heist, the active countdown in Gem Grab, the goal that can close out Brawl Ball. Most frequent mistake Using the gadget reactively against any threat instead of saving it for the moment of maximum impact. The gadget used in the first uncomfortable situation of the match leaves the player without this resource during the critical moments at the end of the match, where the difference between having it and not having it can be the victory.

orig_title: Matchups clave analizados
title: Key matchups analyzed
body:
The following matchups are some of the most frequent and most misunderstood in the game. For each one, the mechanical reason for the advantage and how it should be played from both sides are analyzed.

**Bull vs Piper **[ADVANTAGE]
**Why mechanically: **Bull needs to get to close range where Piper has no safe firing angle. Piper deals massive damage at long range but damage drops drastically at close range. If Bull closes in, Piper cannot eliminate Bull before Bull eliminates her. **How to play this matchup: **Advance using all available map cover. The goal is not the direct duel but reaching close range where the advantage is absolute. Patience is Bull's real mechanic against Piper: don't expose yourself in open ground. **If you can't switch: **If you're on a completely open map with no cover, the matchup reverses: Piper can deal full damage before you arrive. In that case, use Bull's Gadget to absorb the first shot and close the distance as fast as possible.
**Piper vs Mortis **[DISADVANTAGE]
**Why mechanically: **Mortis has enough mobility to close the distance quickly and Piper cannot deal full damage from her charged attack at close range. Once Mortis reaches range, Piper has no short-range answer. **How to play this matchup: **Always prioritize maintaining maximum distance. Never position yourself in areas with no escape route. Use Piper's Gadget to create distance when Mortis begins the approach, not when he is already on top of you. **If you can't switch: **If the map has enough cover for Mortis to always get close, the matchup is structurally lost. Focus on not dying to avoid donating stars (in Bounty) or on having your allies intercept Mortis before he reaches you.
**Emz vs Brock **[ADVANTAGE]
**Why mechanically: **Emz has an area damage field that acts over a wide radius without needing direct aim. Brock needs line of sight and charge time to deal his maximum damage. Emz can operate at mid range where her damage field reaches Brock before Brock can move out of the area. **How to play this matchup: **Position yourself at the edge of Emz's range: close enough for the damage field to reach Brock but far enough that Brock doesn't have time to react to your Super. Use Emz's Super to deny Brock's repositioning space. **If you can't switch: **If Brock has lateral cover to fire from angles where your damage field doesn't reach, the matchup evens out. In that case, rotate to a position where you can cover Brock's angle with your field.
**Frank vs Dynamike **[DISADVANTAGE]
**Why mechanically: **Dynamike can attack from cover with arc projectiles that don't require line of sight. Frank is slow and cannot reach Dynamike if he keeps a wall between them. Frank's Super (stun) requires getting into Dynamike's range, at which point Dynamike can already dodge. **How to play this matchup: **Actively look for an approach angle where you can reach Dynamike without the wall between the two of you. Frank needs to commit Dynamike's position: if Dynamike is trapped in a dead-end zone, Frank can pressure him with the threat of the Super. **If you can't switch: **On a map with high wall density where Dynamike always has cover, the matchup is structurally lost for Frank. Delegate Dynamike's elimination to an ally with more mobility and focus on your Tank role without chasing the Thrower.
**Leon vs Sandy **[BALANCED]
**Why mechanically: **Leon can turn invisible to approach Sandy. Sandy can create a fog zone that makes all allies in the area invisible, including herself, and that puts Leon to sleep if he enters the Super's area. The matchup depends on who uses their Super first and in what context. **How to play this matchup: **As Leon: don't go in until Sandy has used her Super. With Sandy's Super active, Leon enters a zone where he can be put to sleep and lose invisibility. Wait for Sandy's Super to expire and then go in with your invisibility active. **If you can't switch: **If Sandy has allies who can shoot at you while Sandy puts you to sleep, the matchup becomes a disadvantage. In that case, don't attempt the 1v1 duel against Sandy when her allies are in a supporting position.

orig_title: El ciclo del meta: como evoluciona un parche
title: The meta cycle: how a patch evolves
body:
Every time Supercell releases an update, the meta goes through a predictable four-phase cycle. Understanding which phase the meta is in at any given moment allows you to make more informed pick decisions.

1 Initial explosion (days 1-3 post-patch) The community quickly discovers which Brawlers have received direct buffs and brings them to the top of usage. Winrates are erratic because everyone is experimenting: buffed ones rise, nerfed ones fall, but many picks are suboptimal because no one has had time to optimize. It is the moment of greatest noise and least signal. **Concrete action: **Do not make meta decisions during this phase. Observe and wait. Brawlers that seem broken on patch day often balance out once the community discovers their natural counterpicks.
2 Consolidation (days 4-10) High-level players begin to optimize compositions with new or buffed Brawlers. The first trios and compositions that exploit the patch changes start to emerge. The natural counterpicks of meta Brawlers begin appearing more frequently. **Concrete action: **This is the optimal learning phase. Analyze the compositions that are winning: not the individual Brawler, but the complete trio. The composition pattern is the most valuable information of this phase.
3 Meta maturity (days 11-21) The meta stabilizes. The dominant Brawlers are known, their counterpicks are identified, and the optimal compositions are relatively agreed upon among high-rank players. The mature meta is the moment when the tier list has the most accuracy. **Concrete action: **Learn the Brawlers and compositions of the mature meta. If you want to climb in rank, this is the time to pick Brawlers with the best winrates and mastery in the current context.
4 Exhaustion and anticipation (days 22+) The meta's counterpicks are fully optimized. The meta begins to turn on itself: the meta's counterpicks become so common that the original meta Brawlers lose effectiveness, players look for off-meta picks to surprise the counterpickers, and everyone starts anticipating the next patch. **Concrete action: **If you play at high ranks, this is the phase where meta knowledge gives you the least advantage: everyone knows it equally well. Focus on execution and on preparing yourself for the next cycle.

orig_title: Cómo Identificar el Rol de un Brawler Desconocido
title: How to Identify the Role of an Unknown Brawler
body:
When you encounter a Brawler you don't know well, use this four-question mental process to classify them before your next match:

1 At what range does it deal damage? Short
→ Tank/Assassin. Medium
→ DPS/Hybrid. Long
→ Sniper/Thrower
2 How much health does it have? High health + short range = Tank. Low health + high damage = Assassin or Sniper
3 Does it have active mobility? Dash, teleport or high speed indicates Assassin or Support (Max). No mobility = Sniper/Thrower/Tank
4 Does its Super affect the team or enemies? Affects allies
→ Support. Damage/control area
→ Controller. Eliminates enemies
→ DPS/Assassin

orig_title: Tipos de Mapas y su Influencia Táctica
title: Types of Maps and Their Tactical Influence
body: The map is not just the setting where the match takes place: it is the match itself. Two teams with the same Brawlers will play in a completely different way on an open map versus one filled with walls. Understanding the map before choosing a Brawler —or before deciding how to play it— is the most undervalued tactical variable among mid-rank players.

orig_title: El Efecto del Mapa sobre Cada Rol
title: The Effect of the Map on Each Role
body:
The following matrix crosses the four map archetypes with the eight roles from Section 1, assigning a suitability level. Use it as a quick reference when choosing a Brawler or analyzing why a team is losing.

Role Open Closed Lane Mixed
🛡️ Tank ⚠️ Limited ✅ Optimal ⚠️ Situational ✅ Good
🎯 Sniper ✅ Optimal ❌ Very weak ✅ Good ⚠️ Situational
⚔️ DPS ✅ Good ⚠️ Limited ✅ Optimal ✅ Good
🗡️ Assassin ❌ Very weak ✅ Optimal ⚠️ Limited ⚠️ Situational
💚 Support ⚠️ Situational ✅ Good ✅ Optimal ✅ Optimal
💣 Thrower ❌ Very weak ✅ Optimal ✅ Good ✅ Good
🎮 Controller ✅ Good ⚠️ Limited ✅ Good ✅ Optimal
🔀 Hybrid ⚠️ Situational ⚠️ Situational ✅ Good ✅ Optimal
✅ Optimal — Maximum performance ✅ Good — Solid performance ⚠️ Situational — Depends on the specific map ❌ Very weak — Avoid unless strong synergy

orig_title: La pregunta correcta antes de cada partida
title: The right question before each match
body:
With the three sections of Block I assimilated, before starting any match you should be able to answer these three questions in the first few seconds:

# Question What it answers
1 What is the real win condition of this mode? Defines which actions generate points and which are irrelevant. Avoids prioritizing kills when the mode does not reward them.
2 What map archetype do I have and what roles does it favor? Determines which Brawlers are structurally viable and which are at a disadvantage regardless of who plays them.
3 What role does my team need and can my Brawler fulfill it? Closes the triangle of Mode, Map and Role and tells you whether you should play aggressive, defensive, as support or as zone control.
BLOCK I COMPLETED You now have the complete strategic language Roles · Maps · Modes — The triangle that defines every match
Section 1 You know the 8 roles, their strengths, their weaknesses and how to identify any Brawler's role. Section 2 You understand the 4 map archetypes and how each one empowers or neutralizes different roles. Section 3 You master the real win condition of each mode and know which actions generate or waste value.
Next: Block II — Team Theory
With the three fundamentals assimilated, Block II will answer the central question of the guide: How is a team composition built and read in a systematic way? Section 4: Roles by Game Mode · Section 5: Formations · Section 6: Synergies

orig_title: Que hacer cuando el equipo tiene un hueco critico
title: What to do when the team has a critical gap
body:
In random team matches, it is inevitable to encounter compositions with critical role gaps. You cannot always change your Brawler. Here are the most effective compensation strategies based on the detected gap:

Detected gap What can happen How to compensate
No Support in Gem Grab The gem carrier dies as soon as the countdown activates and the opponent focuses them. The player with the most gems must retreat BEFORE the countdown to a safe area. More distance = less need for healing.
No Tank in Hot Zone Nobody can hold inside the zone under sustained pressure. Rotate in a coordinated way: two players in the zone alternating to prevent a single one from absorbing all the damage.
No DPS in Heist The team does not deal enough damage to the safe within the available time. Prioritize eliminating defenders quickly so that lower-damage Brawlers reach the safe without resistance.
No Sniper in Bounty The team cannot eliminate high-value targets from a safe distance. Play more defensively, protect the ally with the most stars, and wait for the opponent to make positioning mistakes.
No zone control in Knockout Without long-range pressure, the opponent controls the pace of combat. Use map cover to close the combat distance and force exchanges at ranges where the team has the advantage.
Connection with Section 5
Now that you know which roles each mode requires, Section 5 will show how those roles combine into composition archetypes: team formations with their own name, logic, and win conditions. A composition is not just about 'covering all roles'. It is about choosing how to distribute them so that the team has a coherent tactical identity.

orig_title: Leer la composicion rival
title: Read the enemy composition
body:
Identifying the enemy archetype allows you to anticipate their strategy and adjust yours. The process is the same as building your own: observe the three enemy Brawlers and determine their tactical identity.

The four questions to read the enemy composition
1. What combat range are they looking for? If all three are short-range, you can beat them by staying far away. If they are long-range, you need cover to get close. 2. Do they have a carry? Identifying the enemy carry and focusing them first breaks their composition. Without a carry, the Protect the Carry archetype collapses. 3. Do they have a Support? If the enemy has an active Support, Full Aggro rarely works: the healing interrupts the kill chain before it is completed. 4. What objective are they pursuing? A Full Aggro team that ignores the zone can be exploited by letting them win the trades while you control the objective.

orig_title: Como identificar sinergias propias
title: How to identify your own synergies
body:
Beyond memorizing specific pairs and trios, the goal of this section is for you to be able to identify synergies on your own given any combination of Brawlers. The following three-question process lets you evaluate whether two Brawlers have real synergy or merely passive coexistence:

Question 1: Does A's action make B's action easier? If the answer is yes, there is an enabling or amplifying synergy. Define the 'maximum value action' of each Brawler and ask whether one sets up the conditions for the other to execute it with a higher probability of success. Example: Frank's Super stuns several enemies. During the stun, allies can shoot without the enemy moving or retaliating. Any Sniper or long-range DPS has their maximum value action (shooting without response) enabled by Frank.
Question 2: Is A's weakness B's strength and vice versa? If the answer is yes, there is a mutual coverage synergy. Identify the main weakness of each Brawler (vulnerability at close range, lack of mobility, dependence on line of sight) and verify whether the other naturally covers it. Example: Piper is destroyed at close range but dominates at long range. Bull dominates at close range and can eliminate anything that approaches Piper. Bull cannot operate at long range, but Piper covers that range. Complete mutual coverage.
Question 3: Does playing A well help the other charge their Super faster? If the answer is yes, there is a Super Chain synergy. Supers in Brawl Stars are the most valuable resource in the match. A pair that generates Supers mutually faster than average has a resource advantage that accumulates over time. Example: Nita summons Bruce. Bruce takes damage, which charges Nita's Super quickly (the more damage Bruce receives, the faster Nita charges). Additionally, Bruce deals damage to enemies, which charges the third ally's Super if that ally is a DPS who needs to hit in order to charge.

orig_title: Adaptacion dinamica: cuando tu rol cambia durante la partida
title: Dynamic adaptation: when your role changes during the match
body:
Role reading is not static. During the match, the context changes and your role may need to adjust. These are the most common moments when the role changes and how to detect them:

What has changed What it means for your role How to adjust
Your allied Tank has died The team has lost its frontline. No one can absorb close-range damage or open space. Fall back to a more conservative position. If you are a DPS, increase your combat distance. If you are a Support, cover the space the Tank occupied until they respawn.
The opponent has switched to a very aggressive playstyle The team is under constant pressure and cannot execute their win condition. Identify the most dangerous opponent and prioritize their elimination over any other objective. A kill at the critical moment can change the pace of the match.
The gem counter has activated (Gem Grab) The team enters defensive mode. Kills are no longer the objective: protecting the carrier is the only objective. Regardless of your previous role, switch to escort mode for the carrier. Your function is to deny the opponent access to the carrier, not to attack.
The team is losing by a significant margin A passive or balanced pace is not enough to make a comeback. Increase your risk level in a calculated way. Look for the asymmetric trade: a kill that generates a numerical advantage or a hit on the objective that reduces the gap. Do not increase risk randomly.
The opponent has a carry with many stars (Bounty) The opponent has a high-value target that, if eliminated, completely changes the scoreboard. Redirect your attention to the enemy carry. A high-value kill is more profitable than two low-value kills. Coordinate with positioning: flank the enemy carry while your ally pressures them from the front.

orig_title: Leer el estado posicional del mapa en tiempo real
title: Reading the positional state of the map in real time
body:
During the match, the positional state changes constantly. Knowing how to read that state allows you to anticipate what is going to happen before it occurs and make movement decisions with an informational advantage over the opponent.

The four positional states and what they imply
OWN POSITIONAL ADVANTAGE: your team controls the objective zone and the dominant flanks. Action: consolidate the position, do not overextend. Overextending from a positional advantage is the most common cause of opponent comebacks. POSITIONAL EQUALITY: both teams control equivalent zones. Action: identify the most unprotected high-value zone of the opponent and orient your next movement toward it. Whoever breaks the balance first has the initiative. POSITIONAL DISADVANTAGE: the opponent controls more high-value zones than your team. Action: do not try to recover everything at once. Identify the easiest high-value zone to recover and concentrate the team's resources on that zone. Recovering position requires a temporary numerical advantage. POSITIONAL COLLAPSE: the opponent controls the objective and the flanks. Action: survive, recharge resources and wait for the moment of the counterattack when the opponent overextends their position. The opponent who controls everything tends to become aggressive and make overextension mistakes.

orig_title: La vida: el recurso mas infravalorado
title: Health: the most underrated resource
body:
Health is not just an indicator of how much damage you can absorb before dying. It is the resource that determines how long you can keep operating in a position of value. A Brawler at 20% health who stays on the front line is handing the enemy a free kill: their damage potential no longer justifies the risk of exposure.

HEALTH (Health Economy) A continuous resource that regenerates slowly outside of combat. Determines how long you can operate without retreating.
When to save it When you have less than 30% health and are still in an active combat position: you are an easy kill handing a numerical advantage to the enemy. When the enemy has their Super charged and you can be the target: dying with the enemy's Super aimed at you is the most avoidable situation in the game. When your team has the advantage and does not need your presence to maintain it: if the team is up 3v2 with high health, retreating to recover health is more valuable than staying exposed at 15%. When briefly stepping away from combat allows you to regenerate enough health to return with a real capacity for impact. When to use it When you have enough health to absorb at least two exchanges: do not retreat out of caution if you can withstand the pressure. When your presence in position is essential even with low health: the gem carrier with an active counter cannot retreat even at 25% health. When an ally is actively healing you and health is recovering in position: the sustain of an active Support allows you to operate with less health than normal. When the play is high-impact and the risk of dying is justified by what you can achieve. Most common mistake Staying in a combat position with critically low health out of unwillingness to give up the position. The player who keeps fighting at 15% health almost always dies and hands the numerical advantage to the enemy. Retreating at 20-25% health, recovering, and returning at 70-80% is systematically more valuable than staying and dying.

orig_title: Estrategias para compensar un contrapick forzado
title: Strategies to compensate a forced counterpick
body:
In modes without bans and without the possibility of switching Brawlers, you will frequently find yourself in unfavorable matchups. These strategies do not eliminate the mechanical disadvantage but reduce it to manageable levels:

Strategy What it consists of When to apply it
Avoiding the duel Do not seek direct confrontation with the Brawler that counterpicks you. Operate in a different area of the map where that rival has no presence or where the map neutralizes their advantage. When the disadvantage is one of range and the map has zones where the duel cannot occur. An Assassin against a Sniper can avoid the duel by staying in the side lanes with cover.
Delegating the matchup Implicitly asking an ally with an advantage in that matchup to deal with the rival that counterpicks you, while you handle the rivals where you have the advantage or an even footing. When an ally has a clear advantage over the rival that counters you. It requires a degree of implicit coordination: positioning yourself so that the ally has a line toward that rival while you apply pressure from another angle.
Using the map against the advantage Actively seeking the areas of the map where the rival's mechanical advantage is neutralized. A Sniper loses their advantage on a closed map; a Thrower loses their advantage in open ground. When the map has distinct zones with different levels of cover density. Move toward the zone that nullifies the rival's advantage even if it is not your generally optimal position.
Super as an equalizer Saving your Super for the moment of the duel against the counterpick and using it to mechanically even out the disadvantage. A well-timed Super at the critical moment can turn a losing matchup into a won trade. When your Brawler's Super has an effect that directly neutralizes the rival's advantage: invulnerability, movement control, instant damage. Do not use your Super before the duel; save it for the exact moment.
Indirect pressure Instead of direct duels, directing all your effort toward the mode's objective. If the rival that counterpicks you is busy eliminating you, they are not on the objective. Sacrificing the duel to win the objective is a valid way to compensate for the disadvantage. In modes where the objective carries more weight than kills: Gem Grab, Hot Zone, Brawl Ball. If the rival eliminates you but your team scores a goal or activates the counter in the meantime, the death has had strategic value.

orig_title: Como construir tu propia lectura del meta
title: How to build your own meta reading
body:
Instead of passively consuming tier lists, the following process allows you to build your own reading of the current meta in less than 20 minutes using publicly available information:

Step 1: Read the current patch notes analytically Don't look for which Brawlers went up or down: look for which mechanics have changed. A buff to a Brawler's base damage has different implications than a buff to their movement speed or Super. Ask yourself: what matchups does this adjustment change? What role is affected? What synergy is strengthened or weakened?
Step 2: Identify the active maps in each mode Look at the maps in rotation and classify them according to Section 2 (open, closed, lane-based, mixed). If most active maps in Gem Grab this week are closed, Throwers will have greater value. If they are open, Snipers and long-range DPS rise. This reading takes two minutes and can completely change your Brawler decision.
Step 3: Analyze the compositions of top-ranked players Players at the top of the trophy leaderboard don't always have the best individual mechanics, but they do have the best picks for the current meta. Observe the trios that repeat, not the individual Brawlers. The composition pattern is the most valuable meta information that exists.
Step 4: Verify with your own in-game experience No meta reading is complete without in-game validation. Play the Brawlers that the analysis suggests and observe: are you winning the matchups that should be favorable? Are the compositions that are supposed to work generating the expected results? If not, the analysis has an error or the context of your rank is different from that of the top.

orig_title: Errores Comunes al Interpretar Roles
title: Common Mistakes When Playing Roles
body:
**⚠️ Error 1: Confusing 'deals a lot of damage' with **'is a good DPS'
A Brawler like Piper deals enormous damage, but only at long range. On a closed map, that damage never materializes. A role is not just a statistic: it is the context in which that statistic becomes useful.
⚠️ Error 2: Assuming the Tank always goes to the front
A Tank without team support is an easy target. The Tank 'opens space' only when the team is in position to take advantage of that space. Alone, the Tank dies without having generated any value.
⚠️ Error 3: Playing the Assassin like a DPS
Assassins have very short time windows to act. If you use Mortis as if he were a frontline DPS, you waste his only differential attribute: the ability to dive in, eliminate, and get out. Patience and timing are the Assassin's real mechanic.
⚠️ Error 4: Ignoring the Thrower's role on open maps
Throwers are one of the most map-dependent roles. On a map without walls, a Barley or Dynamike have the same effective range as any DPS, but with far less damage per direct projectile. The Thrower's value is range over obstacles, not raw damage.

orig_title: Cómo Cambia el Mapa con la Rotación de Modos
title: How the Map Changes with the Mode Rotation
body:
One aspect that many players overlook is that the same map can behave in a tactically different way depending on the game mode assigned to it. A map with an open central zone in Gem Grab creates a very different objective control dynamic than if that same map were used in Brawl Ball, where the objective moves and teams need to push forward.

Key principle: The game mode recontextualizes the map
The value of a position on the map is not absolute: it depends on the mode's objective. A bush zone near the center is decisive in Gem Grab (generator control) but almost irrelevant in Bounty (where the zone doesn't matter, only kills do). Before analyzing a map, always ask yourself: 'What position do I want to control to win this mode?' The answer completely changes the map you are reading.

orig_title: Modos de Juego: Mecanicas, Objetivos y Prioridades
title: Game Modes: Mechanics, Objectives and Priorities
body: Brawl Stars is not a game about killing enemies: it is a game about winning the game mode. This distinction, which seems obvious, is the most widespread conceptual error among mid-ranked players. Each mode defines its own win condition, and understanding that win condition is the third pillar of strategic thinking in Block I.

Connection to Section 6
Composition archetypes define the framework. The synergies between specific Brawlers within that framework define whether the team actually works or only works in theory. Section 6 will go down to the level of specific pairs and trios: which Brawlers enhance each other, why mechanically, and under what conditions that synergy is activated or broken.

orig_title: Sinergias aparentes que no funcionan
title: Apparent synergies that don't work
body:
Not every combination that seems logical proves effective in a match. These are the most common pseudo-synergies that lead to suboptimal compositions:

Combination Why it seems like synergy Why it is not
Triple Sniper Three Snipers deal more long-range damage than any other trio. Without physical presence near the objective, the team cannot control any zone or objective. On maps with bushes, Assassins close in without taking damage. Without a kill threat at range, long-range damage generates no useful advantage.
Triple Support Three Supports generate massive healing and the team never dies. Without damage, the team cannot eliminate enemies or pressure the objective. The opponent can ignore them and execute the objective without resistance. Survival without damage scores no points in any mode.
Two Carries without a Support Two carries deal double damage and offensive pressure. Without a Support, both carries fall before completing their highest-value action. In Gem Grab, the gem carrier dies as soon as the opponent focuses them. A double carry without glue is more fragile than a well-protected single carry.
Leon + Sandy (without a third damage dealer) Cross-invisibility seems unbeatable. Two of the team's three Brawlers are control and positioning oriented. Without a third Brawler with high damage to capitalize on the conditions they create, the synergy does not translate into a real advantage: the opponent survives the fog and the pair cannot close kills.
Two Throwers on an open map Double cross-area damage from a covered position. Throwers only work with walls that give them an angle. On an open map, their arcing projectiles are slower and more predictable than any direct DPS. Two Throwers in open field are two slow DPS Brawlers with very little direct damage per projectile.
BLOCK II COMPLETED You now know how a team functions as a system Roles by Mode · Compositions · Synergies — The team triangle
Section 4 You know which roles each mode needs and how to compensate for critical gaps in random teams. Section 5 You know the 6 composition archetypes, their win conditions, and how to read the opponent's composition. Section 6 You understand the 4 types of synergy and can evaluate whether two Brawlers truly boost each other or merely coexist.
Next: Block III — Practical Application
Blocks I and II have established the complete theoretical framework: roles, maps, modes, compositions, and synergies. Block III brings all that knowledge into the match itself: how to read your role in a random team, how to position yourself, when to use your resources, and how to make decisions in real time. Section 7: Reading Your Role in a Random Team · Section 8: Positioning · Section 9: Resource Management

orig_title: El principio de la accion minima eficaz
title: The Principle of Minimum Effective Action
body:
One of the most common mistakes in random teams is doing too much: trying to cover all roles, attack every opponent, and be everywhere at once. The result is not doing anything well.

The Minimum Effective Action Principle
At every moment of the match, there is a single action that generates the maximum value given your role and the current state of the map. Your goal is to identify that action and execute it well, not try to do everything at once. For a Support with an active counter: healing the carrier is the minimum effective action. Not attacking, not flanking, not looking for kills. Just heal the carrier. For a DPS with an ally under pressure: eliminating the opponent pressuring your ally is the minimum effective action. Not pushing forward, not looking for secondary objectives. Ask yourself at every moment: what is THE ONE THING I should be doing right now? Execute that one thing. Everything else is noise.
Connection to Section 8
Once you know your role and have a clear idea of which action you need to execute, the next question is: from where? Positioning is the variable that turns a good decision into an effective execution. Section 8 develops map control, value zones, and the concept of lane pressure: the mechanisms that determine from which position you can execute your role with maximum impact.

orig_title: Cuando avanzar y cuando ceder: la regla del coste-beneficio
title: When to push forward and when to give ground: the cost-benefit rule
body:
Every forward move has a cost (exposure to damage, moving away from cover) and a benefit (pressure on the opponent, access to the objective). The rule is simple: advance when the benefit outweighs the cost; give ground when the cost outweighs the benefit.

Advance when... Give ground when...
You have a numerical advantage (3v2 or 3v1) and open space is your ally. Your Super is charged and pushing forward puts you in range to use it effectively. The opponent just died or fell back and the space ahead is temporarily free. Advancing places you in the objective zone while the counter is active. Your ally Tank has already created the space: push forward to consolidate it before the opponent recovers their position. You are at a numerical disadvantage (2v3 or 1v3): pushing forward only generates more deaths. Your Super is not charged and you need to receive it before committing. The opponent has a clean line of fire toward your advancing position: the cost is too high. Giving up the center brings you closer to your base where your allies can cover you. The mode does not reward an offensive position at this moment (opponent's counter active: do not advance, deny access).

orig_title: Los tres recursos como sistema
title: The three resources as a system
body:
The Super, the Gadget, and health are not managed independently: they are an interconnected system where a decision about one affects the other two. These are the most important management patterns:

Pattern How it works When to apply it
Super to create health space Using the Super not to kill but to create distance or invulnerability that allows you to recover health. Examples: Mortis's dash Super to escape, Rosa's shield Super to tank damage while regenerating. When health is critical and the Gadget has already been used. Using the Super as a survival tool is a valid decision if no equivalent offensive use is available. When health is critical and the Gadget has already been used. Using the Super as a survival tool is a valid decision if no equivalent offensive use is available.
Gadget to protect the Super Using the Gadget to survive long enough to reach the optimal moment to use the Super. A Gadget charge "wasted" on survival may be the reason the Super arrives fully charged at the critical moment. When the Super is charged but the opponent has a play set up to prevent you from using it. The Gadget that interrupts that play protects the value of the Super. When the Super is charged but the opponent has a play set up to prevent you from using it. The Gadget that interrupts that play protects the value of the Super.
Health as a shield for resources Maintaining enough health to be able to use the Super and the Gadget at their optimal moment without a premature death wasting them. A Brawler who dies with the Super charged and the Gadget unused has not only lost a life: they have lost all three resources at once. Always. Health is the prerequisite for being able to use the other two resources. If you die, you lose the Super and the Gadget without having made use of them. Always. Health is the prerequisite for being able to use the other two resources. If you die, you lose the Super and the Gadget without having made use of them.

orig_title: Pensamiento critico sobre los tier lists
title: Critical thinking about tier lists
body:
Tier lists are useful tools as a starting point, but they have structural limitations that make them dangerous if used as the only source of decision-making:

Tier list limitation How to compensate for it
They are not mode- or map-specific A Brawler that is A-tier in general may be D-tier in the mode you are playing with the active map this week. Always filter the tier list by mode and by map archetype before using it as a pick guide.
They reflect the meta of whoever makes them, not yours A tier list made by a Masters player reflects what works in Masters. In Gold or Diamond ranks, counterpicks are not the same and the learning curve penalizes differently. Look for tier lists specific to your rank.
They always lag behind the actual meta A tier list takes days or weeks to update after a patch. If you learn to read patches directly, your meta reading will always be one week ahead of the most up-to-date tier list.
They hide the reason behind the ranking Knowing that Bull is A-tier does not tell you why. Without understanding the reason (he dominates on closed Brawl Ball maps post-buff to his speed), you cannot know whether Bull will remain A-tier when the maps change.
They promote reactive play Following the tier list makes you react to the meta instead of anticipating it. A player who understands why a Brawler is meta can identify the next meta Brawler before the tier list reflects it and get ahead of the adoption curve before everyone else.

orig_title: BLOQUE II · TEORIA DE EQUIPO
title: BLOCK II · TEAM THEORY

orig_title: Los tres errores posicionales mas frecuentes
title: The three most frequent positional mistakes
body:
Positional Error 1 — Overextension
Advancing beyond the point where your team can cover you. Overextension occurs when a player sees the opponent retreat and chases them beyond the support radius of their allies. The retreating opponent may be doing so deliberately to lure you into a position where your allies cannot help you. Rule: if your allies cannot see you or reach you within two seconds, you are overextended.
Positional Error 2 — Objective Tunnel Vision
Focusing so intently on the objective (the safe, the goal, the gems) that you ignore the opponent's positional threats. Objective tunnel vision is especially common in Heist and Brawl Ball: the player who only watches the safe or the goal does not see the Assassin who has been flanking them for three seconds. Fix it with habit: every three seconds, check where all three opponents are, not just where the objective is.
Positional Error 3 — Mirror Positioning
Copying an ally's position instead of complementing it. If your DPS ally is already in the right lane, going to the same lane does not double the pressure: it doubles the exposure. The opponent can concentrate their fire on a single point and clear both of you. Correct positioning is always complementary: if an ally holds the right lane, you take the left or the center, forcing the opponent to defend two points simultaneously.
Connection with Section 9
Positioning defines where you act from. Resource management defines what you act with. Section 9 closes Block III with the third axis of in-game execution: when to use the Super, when to use the Gadget, and how to manage your own health to maximize the time you can operate in a position of value.

orig_title: El ciclo de recursos: leer el estado del equipo rival
title: The resource cycle: reading the enemy team's state
body:
Managing your resources does not happen in a vacuum: it happens in relation to the state of the opponent's resources. Knowing whether the opponent has their super charged or not is tactical information just as valuable as knowing where they are positioned.

How to read the opponent's resource state
Opponent's super charged: the opponent has a full or nearly full bar. Signs: they have attacked a lot without dying, have taken a lot of damage, or you have not seen them use their super in a long time. Action: do not expose yourself, fall back to a lower-risk position until they use it. Opponent's super drained: they just used their super or have not been in the match long. Action: this is the window of maximum pressure. The opponent cannot respond with their most powerful resource for the next few seconds. Take advantage to push forward or execute on the objective. Opponent's Gadgets: in the late stage of the match, assuming the opponent has spent one or two Gadgets reduces uncertainty. If the opponent has already used their defensive Gadget, your offensive play has a higher chance of success. Opponent's health: an opponent with low health who is not retreating is either taking a calculated risk or has made a mistake. It is the moment to apply pressure: the cost of their next death is high and their ability to respond is limited.
